ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef steak food product class; no specific blade steak subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers grilled beef meat broadly; no blade-cut-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for grilled beef blade steak identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled beef blade steak is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium steak portion as commonly served at a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Beef, blade steak, grilled (cr_id F06030022)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06030022 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core per_100g values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat (2.9 g), monounsaturated fat (3.1 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.3 g), and trans fat (0.1 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 (beef, chuck, blade roast, cooked, braised) as the closest approved-source equivalent for fatty acid fractions not separately reported by HPB for this entry; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F06030022) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled beef blade steak contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F06030022, confidence 0.95, reviewed) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load; foods with negligible carbohydrate are excluded from GI classification. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (46 mg/100g) and high potassium (380 mg/100g) yield a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled beef blade steak is suitable for hypertensive individuals when consumed without added salt or high-sodium sauces; limit portion to 100–150 g to manage total saturated fat intake (~2.9 g/100g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g) and zero sugar (0.0 g/100g) mean this food does not raise postprandial blood glucose; high protein (30.5 g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef blade steak is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate protein source; avoid sweet marinades or glazes that add sugar, and pair with non-starchy vegetables per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.9 g/100g) and cholesterol (80 mg/100g) are present at moderate levels; naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) is also present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled beef blade steak to 1–2 servings per week, keep portions to 100 g, avoid added saturated-fat sauces, and balance intake with omega-3-rich fish and plant proteins per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in red meat amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) is also present and is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week per EULAR RA dietary guidelines, pair with anti-inflammatory foods (turmeric, ginger, omega-3-rich fish), and choose lean cut variants where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled beef blade steak is a cut of red meat taken from the shoulder blade (chuck) portion of beef cattle, typically cooked bone-in on a grill or griddle until cooked through, resulting in a slightly charred exterior and juicy interior. The blade cut contains lean muscle meat interspersed with internal and external fat, giving it a rich, beefy flavour and a moderately tender texture when grilled correctly. Per 100 g, it provides 189 kcal, 30.5 g protein, 7.4 g total fat (approximately 2.9 g saturated, 3.1 g monounsaturated, 0.3 g polyunsaturated, and 0.1 g naturally occurring ruminant trans fat), 0.0 g carbohydrate, 0.0 g sugar, 46 mg sodium, 380 mg potassium, 6 mg calcium, 2.46 mg iron, and 80 mg cholesterol. The food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is classified as minimally processed. Clinically, the zero-carbohydrate profile makes it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes and those following ketogenic or low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The low sodium and high potassium content support DASH dietary principles for hypertension management. However, the presence of saturated fat and cholesterol warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and the high arachidonic acid content of red meat makes it unsuitable for frequent consumption by people with rheumatoid arthritis. Grilled beef blade steak is commonly served as a main protein at Western-style meals and is widely consumed across Singapore, Australia, the United Kingdom, and North America.

notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F06030022) — grilled beef blade steak is a pure protein-and-fat food with no carbohydrate content; this is naturally zero for plain grilled beef with no marinade or coating. Sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g: naturally zero. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g: naturally zero as this is an animal-derived muscle meat product. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g); GI testing per University of Sydney GI Database methodology requires a minimum available carbohydrate load and is not performed for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Saturated_fat_g (2.9 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 (beef, chuck, blade roast, cooked, braised) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry; values are plausible for this cut and cooking method. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for grilled beef blade steak are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for cr_id F06030022; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 provides partial micronutrient data but the match type is adapted rather than exact, and these values have not been carried over to avoid overstating precision. This food is a Western generic food; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese multilingual naming requirements apply under G6. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input cultural field is retained in meal_context as a possible but non-primary use case; the primary meal context is main course / dinner.

Fatty acid fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 and documented. Carbohydrate, sugar, and dietary fibre zeros are justified as naturally zero and confirmed zero for plain grilled beef. GI is correctly set to null with gi_category not_applicable; gi_source references approved sources (HPB, USDA) confirming negligible carbohydrate and cites University of Sydney GI Database methodology, satisfying g2 and q4. Ontology anchors are defensible closest_parent matches with gap notes. All three mandatory health conditions present with clinical guidance and correct human_condition_name for Type 2 Diabetes. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias consistency verified — all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array. No multilingual naming requirement applies for this Western generic food.
